title: Tasks, Intents & Window Management
description: Task affinity, launch modes, Intent flag combinations, IntentFilter matching algorithm, data URI resolution, WMS window hierarchy, ViewRootImpl, edge-to-edge insets, and multi-window architecture

references.title: Android Developers — Tasks and Back Stack

lesson:
Task Affinity & Complex Launch Modes

A task is a stack of Activities that the user perceives as a single unit of work. Each task appears as a separate entry in the Recents screen. By default, all Activities in an app share the same task affinity (the app's package name), but you can override this with android:taskAffinity.

Default: All activities share one task ┌────────────────────────────────┐ │ Task (com.example.app) │ │ ┌──────────────────────────┐ │ │ │ DetailActivity (top) │ │ │ ├──────────────────────────┤ │ │ │ ListActivity │ │ │ ├──────────────────────────┤ │ │ │ MainActivity (root) │ │ │ └──────────────────────────┘ │ └────────────────────────────────┘ With taskAffinity + NEW_TASK: ┌────────────────┐ ┌───────────────┐ │ Task 1 (main) │ │ Task 2 (chat) │ │ MainActivity │ │ ChatActivity │ └────────────────┘ └───────────────┘ (Recents entry 1) (Recents entry 2)
Launch ModeBehaviorUse Case
standardNew instance every time, placed on calling taskMost activities
singleTopReuses top instance via onNewIntent(); new instance if not on topSearch results, notifications
singleTaskOnly one instance per task; clears above it when re-activatedHome/dashboard screen
singleInstanceSole activity in its task; always gets its own taskLauncher, call screen
singleInstancePerTaskOne instance per task, but multiple tasks can each have one (API 31+)Multi-window document editors

allowTaskReparenting="true" lets an Activity move between tasks. Example: a browser Activity launched from your app’s task can move to the browser’s task when the browser is next brought forward. This happens because the system matches the Activity’s taskAffinity with the incoming task.

Key insight: taskAffinity alone does nothing. It only takes effect when combined with FLAG_ACTIVITY_NEW_TASK (for launching into a matching task) or allowTaskReparenting="true" (for moving between tasks).

Intent Flags Deep Dive

Intent flags give you runtime control over Activity launch behavior. They override or combine with manifest launchMode declarations:

FlagEffect
FLAG_ACTIVITY_NEW_TASKStart Activity in a task matching its taskAffinity. Required when launching from non-Activity contexts (Service, BroadcastReceiver).
FLAG_ACTIVITY_CLEAR_TOPIf the Activity already exists in the task, destroy everything above it. With standard launchMode: the existing instance is also destroyed and re-created. With singleTop: existing instance gets onNewIntent().
FLAG_ACTIVITY_SINGLE_TOPSame as singleTop launchMode. If the Activity is already at the top of the task, call onNewIntent() instead of creating a new instance.
FLAG_ACTIVITY_CLEAR_TASKClear the entire task before launching the Activity. Must be used with NEW_TASK.
FLAG_ACTIVITY_NO_HISTORYActivity is not kept in the back stack. Once the user leaves, it is finished.
FLAG_ACTIVITY_EXCLUDE_FROM_RECENTSTask does not appear in the Recents screen.
Common trap — CLEAR_TOP + standard launchMode: When FLAG_ACTIVITY_CLEAR_TOP finds the target Activity in the stack, it destroys everything above it including the target itself, then creates a new instance. If you want to reuse the existing instance, also add FLAG_ACTIVITY_SINGLE_TOP. The combination NEW_TASK | CLEAR_TOP | SINGLE_TOP behaves like singleTask.
// Notification deep link pattern: clear the entire task and land on target val intent = Intent(context, DashboardActivity::class.java).apply { flags = Intent.FLAG_ACTIVITY_NEW_TASK or Intent.FLAG_ACTIVITY_CLEAR_TASK } // Navigate to root, reusing existing instance val homeIntent = Intent(context, MainActivity::class.java).apply { flags = Intent.FLAG_ACTIVITY_CLEAR_TOP or Intent.FLAG_ACTIVITY_SINGLE_TOP }
Flag vs manifest precedence: Intent flags generally take precedence over manifest launchMode. However, some manifest attributes (singleInstance) cannot be overridden by flags. When in doubt, flags win for the caller’s intent, but the callee’s manifest restricts what’s possible.

IntentFilter Matching Algorithm

When you call startActivity(implicitIntent), the PackageManagerService (PMS) resolves it by checking all declared <intent-filter> elements. A filter must pass all three tests:

Implicit Intent Resolution ─────────────────────── Intent { action=VIEW, category=DEFAULT, data=https://example.com/products/42 } │ ▼ ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ TEST 1: Action │ │ Intent's action must match ANY one │ │ declared <action> in the filter │ │ (filter must declare at least one action) │ └─────────────────────────────────────────┘ │ pass? ▼ ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ TEST 2: Category │ │ ALL categories in the Intent must be │ │ declared in the filter. │ │ CATEGORY_DEFAULT is auto-added by │ │ startActivity() — filter MUST include it │ └─────────────────────────────────────────┘ │ pass? ▼ ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ TEST 3: Data (scheme + authority + path │ │ + MIME type) │ │ Each part of the URI must match. │ │ MIME wildcards: image/* or */* │ └─────────────────────────────────────────┘ │ pass? ▼ ✅ MATCH
Why CATEGORY_DEFAULT is required: startActivity() and startActivityForResult() automatically add CATEGORY_DEFAULT to the Intent. If your <intent-filter> does not declare <category android:name="android.intent.category.DEFAULT" />, it will never match implicit intents (except for MAIN/LAUNCHER filters, which are special-cased).

Data URIs in Intent Matching

The data test checks the Intent’s URI and MIME type against the filter’s <data> elements:

// URI structure scheme://authority/path https://example.com/products/42 | | | scheme host:port pathPrefix // IntentFilter for HTTPS deep links <intent-filter> <action android:name="android.intent.action.VIEW" /> <category android:name="android.intent.category.DEFAULT" /> <category android:name="android.intent.category.BROWSABLE" /> <data android:scheme="https" android:host="example.com" android:pathPrefix="/products" /> </intent-filter>
Data ElementMatching Rule
schemeExact match (case-insensitive). Must be specified first — without a scheme, all other URI parts are ignored.
host (authority)Exact match or wildcard (*.example.com). Ignored if no scheme.
pathExact match: path, prefix: pathPrefix, pattern: pathPattern (with .* wildcards). Ignored if no scheme + authority.
mimeTypeSupports wildcards: image/*, */*. Can be declared without URI parts (e.g., share intents with content: URIs).
MIME-only vs URI-only filters: A filter with only mimeType matches intents with a content: or file: URI that has the matching MIME type. A filter with only URI parts (no mimeType) matches intents that have a URI but no MIME type. When both are specified, both must match.

Window Hierarchy

The WindowManagerService (WMS) runs in system_server and manages every window on screen. Each window is backed by a Surface — a buffer that receives drawing commands. SurfaceFlinger composites all Surfaces into the final frame displayed on screen.

Window Z-Order (back to front) ────────────────────────────── ┌──────────────────────────┐ Z = highest │ SYSTEM WINDOWS │ │ Status bar, Navigation │ │ bar, Toast, System alert │ ├──────────────────────────┤ │ SUB-WINDOWS │ │ PopupWindow, Dialog, │ │ Menus, Spinner dropdown │ ├──────────────────────────┤ │ APPLICATION WINDOWS │ │ Activities, InputMethod │ │ Dream, Wallpaper │ └──────────────────────────┘ Z = lowest │ SurfaceFlinger composites all Surfaces into the final display frame

Window types are defined in WindowManager.LayoutParams:

Type RangeCategoryExamples
1–99ApplicationActivity windows (TYPE_BASE_APPLICATION), dialogs
1000–1999Sub-windowsTYPE_APPLICATION_PANEL (PopupWindow), TYPE_APPLICATION_MEDIA
2000–2999SystemTYPE_STATUS_BAR, TYPE_NAVIGATION_BAR, TYPE_TOAST, TYPE_SYSTEM_ALERT

ViewRootImpl

ViewRootImpl is the bridge between the View hierarchy and the window system. It is not a View — it implements ViewParent and manages the connection to WMS.

setContentView() Call Chain ──────────────────────────── Activity.setContentView(layoutRes) │ ▼ PhoneWindow.setContentView() │ creates/finds DecorView │ inflates layout into content frame ▼ ActivityThread.handleResumeActivity() │ ▼ WindowManager.addView(decorView, params) │ ▼ WindowManagerGlobal.addView() │ creates ViewRootImpl ▼ ViewRootImpl.setView(decorView) │ └─ IWindowSession.addToDisplayAsUser() → WMS (Binder IPC) │ └─ Creates Surface │ └─ Requests first layout traversal ▼ ViewRootImpl.performTraversals() ├─ measure() ├─ layout() └─ draw() → Surface → SurfaceFlinger

ViewRootImpl responsibilities:

  • Holds the Surface for drawing
  • Schedules traversals via Choreographer on VSYNC signals
  • Dispatches input events (touch, key) from InputDispatcher to the View tree
  • Manages window insets (system bars, IME)
  • Enforces the "only UI thread can touch views" rule via checkThread()
One ViewRootImpl per window: Each window (Activity, Dialog, PopupWindow, Toast) has its own ViewRootImpl. A Dialog creates a separate window with its own ViewRootImpl, distinct from the Activity’s.

Window Insets & Edge-to-Edge

Window insets describe areas of the screen occupied by system UI (status bar, navigation bar, IME, display cutout). Starting with Android 15 (API 35), apps targeting this API go edge-to-edge by default — the system no longer adds padding for system bars automatically.

// Enable edge-to-edge (call in onCreate before setContentView) override fun onCreate(savedInstanceState: Bundle?) { enableEdgeToEdge() // from androidx.activity super.onCreate(savedInstanceState) setContentView(R.layout.activity_main) // Handle insets manually for Views ViewCompat.setOnApplyWindowInsetsListener(findViewById(R.id.root)) { v, insets -> val bars = insets.getInsets(WindowInsetsCompat.Type.systemBars()) v.setPadding(bars.left, bars.top, bars.right, bars.bottom) insets } }
// Compose: handle insets declaratively Scaffold( modifier = Modifier.fillMaxSize() ) { innerPadding -> LazyColumn( modifier = Modifier .fillMaxSize() .padding(innerPadding), contentPadding = WindowInsets.systemBars.asPaddingValues() ) { items(data) { item -> ItemRow(item) } } }
Inset TypeWhat It Covers
systemBars()Status bar + navigation bar + caption bar
ime()Software keyboard
displayCutout()Notch / punch-hole camera area
systemGestures()Gesture navigation edge zones (left/right/bottom)
tappableElement()Areas where system UI intercepts taps
Android 15 breaking change: Apps targeting API 35+ are edge-to-edge by default. If you do not handle insets, your content will draw behind the status bar and navigation bar. There is no opt-out — you must handle insets properly using WindowInsetsCompat or Compose’s WindowInsets.

Multi-Window Architecture

Android supports three multi-window modes, all managed by WMS and the task/activity stack:

ModeAPIBehavior
Split-screen24+Two tasks side-by-side (or top/bottom). User drags divider. Both activities are resumed (multi-resume, API 29+).
Freeform24+Desktop-like windows, resizable. Enabled by device manufacturer. Common on ChromeOS, tablets.
Picture-in-Picture (PiP)26+Small overlay window. Activity enters PiP via enterPictureInPictureMode(). Paused state (pre-API 31) or resumed.
// Entering PiP mode override fun onUserLeaveHint() { if (Build.VERSION.SDK_INT >= Build.VERSION_CODES.O) { enterPictureInPictureMode( PictureInPictureParams.Builder() .setAspectRatio(Rational(16, 9)) .setAutoEnterEnabled(true) // API 31+ .build() ) } }
Multi-window lifecycle trap: When entering split-screen on API 24–28, the Activity is destroyed and re-created (configuration change). On API 29+, multi-resume means both activities are in RESUMED state. Do not assume only one Activity is resumed at a time. Always use onTopResumedActivityChanged() (API 29+) to know if you are the topmost resumed activity.
Resizing considerations: Never hardcode screen dimensions. Use WindowMetricsCalculator from Jetpack WindowManager to get current window size. In multi-window mode, the window size is smaller than the display. Using Display.getSize() or Resources.displayMetrics returns the full display, not your window bounds.

questions.title: Task Affinity + NEW_TASK Behavior
questions.description: An Activity declares a custom taskAffinity and is launched with FLAG_ACTIVITY_NEW_TASK. Where does it end up?
questions.code:
<!-- AndroidManifest.xml -->
<activity
    android:name=".ChatActivity"
    android:taskAffinity="com.example.chat"
    android:launchMode="standard" />

<activity
    android:name=".MainActivity"
    android:taskAffinity="com.example.app" />

// Launching from MainActivity:
val intent = Intent(this, ChatActivity::class.java)
intent.flags = Intent.FLAG_ACTIVITY_NEW_TASK
startActivity(intent)
options: ChatActivity is placed on top of MainActivity in the same task, because they are in the same app
ChatActivity is launched in a separate task with affinity "com.example.chat" — it appears as a separate entry in Recents
The launch fails with an exception because you cannot use NEW_TASK within the same app
ChatActivity replaces MainActivity in the current task
questions.explanation: FLAG_ACTIVITY_NEW_TASK tells the system to find or create a task whose affinity matches the Activity's taskAffinity. Since ChatActivity declares taskAffinity="com.example.chat" (different from the default app affinity), the system creates a new task for it. This new task appears as a separate entry in the Recents screen. Without the differing taskAffinity, NEW_TASK would find the existing task (same affinity) and place the Activity there.
hints: FLAG_ACTIVITY_NEW_TASK looks for a task matching the target Activity's taskAffinity
Different taskAffinity + NEW_TASK = separate Recents entry

learnMore.title: Android Developers — Tasks and Back Stack

questions.title: CLEAR_TOP with Standard vs SingleTop
questions.description: Your task has Activities A → B → C → D (D on top). You launch Activity B with FLAG_ACTIVITY_CLEAR_TOP. What happens differently when B has launchMode="standard" vs launchMode="singleTop"?
options: Both cases: B receives onNewIntent() and C, D are destroyed
standard: B is destroyed and re-created (C, D also destroyed). singleTop: existing B receives onNewIntent() (C, D destroyed)
standard: nothing happens because CLEAR_TOP only works with singleTask. singleTop: B receives onNewIntent()
Both cases: B is destroyed and re-created. The launchMode makes no difference with CLEAR_TOP
questions.explanation: With FLAG_ACTIVITY_CLEAR_TOP, Activities C and D are always destroyed. The critical difference is what happens to B: standard launchMode — the existing instance of B is also destroyed, then a brand-new instance is created (full onCreate()). singleTop launchMode (or adding FLAG_ACTIVITY_SINGLE_TOP) — the existing instance of B is kept alive and receives onNewIntent(). This is because standard mode always creates a new instance, even when CLEAR_TOP clears above.
hints: Think about what 'standard' launchMode means: always a new instance
To get CLEAR_TOP + reuse, you need singleTop behavior

questions.title: IntentFilter for HTTPS Deep Links
questions.description: Complete this IntentFilter to handle HTTPS URLs for example.com/products/* paths.

blanks.hint: Standard action for viewing data / opening a URL
Category that allows the Activity to be launched from a browser link
Attribute that matches a URI path starting with a given prefix

questions.explanation: ACTION_VIEW is the standard action for viewing data, including URLs. CATEGORY_BROWSABLE is required for an Activity to be launchable from a browser — without it, clicking a link in Chrome cannot open your app. pathPrefix matches any path starting with /products (e.g., /products/42, /products/shoes). CATEGORY_DEFAULT is required because startActivity() adds it automatically.
hints: Opening a URL in the browser is 'viewing' data
What category allows a browser to invoke this filter?

questions.title: IntentFilter Three-Test Rule
questions.description: An implicit Intent has action=SEND, categories=[DEFAULT, BROWSABLE], and mimeType=text/plain. Which IntentFilter matches?
options: Filter A: action=SEND, category=DEFAULT (missing BROWSABLE category)
Filter B: action=SEND, category=DEFAULT, category=BROWSABLE, mimeType=text/plain
Filter C: action=SEND, action=VIEW, category=DEFAULT, category=BROWSABLE, mimeType=image/* (wrong MIME type)
Filter D: action=VIEW, category=DEFAULT, category=BROWSABLE, mimeType=text/plain (wrong action)
questions.explanation: IntentFilter matching requires passing all three tests: (1) Action test — the Intent's action must match at least one declared action. Filters B and C have SEND, Filter D only has VIEW (fails). (2) Category test — every category in the Intent must be declared in the filter. Filter A is missing BROWSABLE (fails). (3) Data test — MIME type must match. Filter C declares image/* but the Intent has text/plain (fails). Only Filter B passes all three tests.
hints: Action test: match ANY one. Category test: match ALL. Data test: MIME must match
Filter A fails because BROWSABLE is in the Intent but not the filter

questions.title: Notification Deep Link Creates Duplicate Tasks
questions.description: Users report that tapping a notification always creates a new task in Recents, even if the app is already open. After several notifications, the Recents screen shows many copies of the same app. Fix the PendingIntent so it reuses the existing task.
questions.starterCode:
fun showNotification(context: Context, itemId: String) {
    val intent = Intent(context, DetailActivity::class.java).apply {
        putExtra("item_id", itemId)
        flags = Intent.FLAG_ACTIVITY_NEW_TASK
    }
    val pendingIntent = PendingIntent.getActivity(
        context, 0, intent,
        PendingIntent.FLAG_IMMUTABLE or PendingIntent.FLAG_UPDATE_CURRENT
    )
    val notification = NotificationCompat.Builder(context, CHANNEL_ID)
        .setSmallIcon(R.drawable.ic_notification)
        .setContentTitle("New item available")
        .setContentIntent(pendingIntent)
        .setAutoCancel(true)
        .build()
    NotificationManagerCompat.from(context).notify(itemId.hashCode(), notification)
}

checks.message: Add FLAG_ACTIVITY_CLEAR_TASK to reuse the existing task instead of creating duplicates

questions.solution:
fun showNotification(context: Context, itemId: String) {
    val intent = Intent(context, DetailActivity::class.java).apply {
        putExtra("item_id", itemId)
        flags = Intent.FLAG_ACTIVITY_NEW_TASK or Intent.FLAG_ACTIVITY_CLEAR_TASK
    }
    val pendingIntent = PendingIntent.getActivity(
        context, itemId.hashCode(), intent,
        PendingIntent.FLAG_IMMUTABLE or PendingIntent.FLAG_UPDATE_CURRENT
    )
    val notification = NotificationCompat.Builder(context, CHANNEL_ID)
        .setSmallIcon(R.drawable.ic_notification)
        .setContentTitle("New item available")
        .setContentIntent(pendingIntent)
        .setAutoCancel(true)
        .build()
    NotificationManagerCompat.from(context).notify(itemId.hashCode(), notification)
}
questions.explanation: The bug is using FLAG_ACTIVITY_NEW_TASK alone. This creates a new task every time if the existing task isn't in the foreground with the same affinity. Adding FLAG_ACTIVITY_CLEAR_TASK clears the existing task and places the new Activity as the root, preventing duplicate Recents entries. The combination NEW_TASK | CLEAR_TASK is the standard pattern for notification deep links. Additionally, the requestCode in PendingIntent.getActivity() should be unique per notification to avoid PendingIntent reuse across different items.
hints: FLAG_ACTIVITY_NEW_TASK alone creates a new task if one doesn't already match
What flag clears an existing task so the Activity becomes the new root?

learnMore.title: Android Developers — Create a notification deep link

questions.title: ViewRootImpl in the setContentView() Chain
questions.description: When does ViewRootImpl enter the picture during Activity startup, and what role does it play?
options: ViewRootImpl is created inside setContentView() when the layout XML is inflated
ViewRootImpl is created after onResume() when ActivityThread calls WindowManager.addView() — it connects the DecorView to WMS via Binder and triggers the first measure/layout/draw pass
ViewRootImpl is created during onCreate() to set up the Surface for drawing
ViewRootImpl is a View subclass that sits between DecorView and the content layout
questions.explanation: setContentView() only inflates the layout into the DecorView — no window is created yet. ViewRootImpl is created after onResume() when ActivityThread.handleResumeActivity() calls WindowManager.addView(decorView). At this point, ViewRootImpl: (1) Establishes a Binder connection to WMS via IWindowSession. (2) Creates the Surface. (3) Calls requestLayout() which schedules the first traversal via Choreographer. (4) The traversal performs measure(), layout(), and draw(). ViewRootImpl is not a View — it implements ViewParent and serves as the root parent of the DecorView.
hints: setContentView() just inflates XML into a ViewGroup — no Surface or WMS connection yet
Think about when the Activity's content actually becomes visible on screen

questions.title: Edge-to-Edge with Compose Inset Handling
questions.description: Implement an edge-to-edge Activity with Compose that displays a scrollable list. The list items should not be obscured by the status bar or navigation bar. Use Compose's WindowInsets APIs.

checks.message: Handle system bar insets so content is not obscured
Use LazyColumn for the scrollable list

questions.explanation: enableEdgeToEdge() configures the Activity to draw behind system bars (transparent status bar and navigation bar). Without inset handling, list items would be hidden behind these bars. Scaffold automatically handles insets via its innerPadding. Alternatively, use WindowInsets.systemBars.asPaddingValues() as contentPadding in LazyColumn — this adds inset-aware padding to the scroll content while allowing the background to draw edge-to-edge.
hints: enableEdgeToEdge() replaces the old WindowCompat.setDecorFitsSystemWindows() approach
Scaffold in Material 3 automatically provides inset-safe innerPadding

questions.title: Window Type Z-Ordering
questions.description: Which window type has the highest Z-order (drawn on top of everything else)?
options: Application windows (TYPE_BASE_APPLICATION) — they are the main content
Sub-windows (TYPE_APPLICATION_PANEL) — they overlay their parent Activity
System windows (TYPE_STATUS_BAR, TYPE_NAVIGATION_BAR) — system UI is always on top
All window types share the same Z-order and are composited by their creation time
questions.explanation: Windows are Z-ordered by their type ranges defined in WindowManager.LayoutParams: Application windows (types 1–99) are at the bottom. Sub-windows (1000–1999) are drawn above their parent application window. System windows (2000–2999) have the highest Z-order — the status bar, navigation bar, and system alerts always appear on top. Within each range, individual windows are ordered by their layer assignment. This is why a PopupWindow (sub-window) appears above your Activity, and the status bar (system window) appears above everything.
hints: Think about why the status bar is always visible on top of any app
Window types use numeric ranges: higher range = higher Z-order

questions.title: Multi-Window Crash: Hardcoded Screen Width
questions.description: Users report a crash when entering split-screen mode. The app uses the full display width to calculate a grid column count, but in split-screen the window is only half the display. Fix the code to use the actual window size.

checks.message: Use WindowMetricsCalculator or WindowMetrics to get the actual window size instead of the display size

questions.explanation: The bug: Display.getMetrics() returns the full display size, not the app's window size. In split-screen mode, the window is roughly half the display, so the column count is calculated for the full screen and content overflows. The fix: use WindowMetricsCalculator.computeCurrentWindowMetrics() from the Jetpack WindowManager library, which returns the actual window bounds — correct in multi-window, freeform, and PiP modes. Alternatively, on API 30+, use windowManager.currentWindowMetrics.
hints: In split-screen, the display size and the window size are different
Jetpack WindowManager provides APIs that work correctly in multi-window

questions.title: Activity Lifecycle in Split-Screen
questions.description: On API 29+, what happens to your Activity's lifecycle when the user puts it into split-screen mode alongside another app?
options: Your Activity is paused (onPause) because it no longer has full focus
Your Activity is destroyed and re-created due to a configuration change (screen size), then resumes — and both split-screen activities are in the RESUMED state simultaneously (multi-resume)
Your Activity stays in RESUMED state without any lifecycle changes
Your Activity is stopped (onStop) because it is partially hidden by the other app
questions.explanation: Entering split-screen triggers a configuration change (screenSize, smallestScreenSize) because the window dimensions change. Unless you handle these in android:configChanges, the Activity is destroyed and re-created. After re-creation, on API 29+ with multi-resume, both split-screen Activities enter the RESUMED state simultaneously. Before API 29, only the focused Activity was RESUMED; the other was PAUSED. Use onTopResumedActivityChanged(boolean) (API 29+) to detect which Activity has topmost focus for exclusive resources like the camera.
hints: Screen size changes are configuration changes by default
Multi-resume (API 29+) changed how split-screen lifecycle works
